coredata.dc:title: Synergistic effect of barium titanate nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots on the dielectric properties and conductivity of poly(vinylidenefluoride-co-hexafluoroethylene) films
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar, Y. Ravi
Khadheer Pasha, S.K.
coredata.dc:description:
PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs polymer nanocomposite films with good flexibility and high dielectric constant (ϵ) at low frequency were prepared via solution casting technique. Different compositions of BaTiO3 (non-conducting ceramic nanofiller) and Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (conducting nanofiller) utilized as co-filler were embedded in poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoroethylene) (PVDF-HFP) polymer matrix. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) techniques were employed to characterize the prepared nanocomposite films. The mechanical properties of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films were also investigated. Dielectric properties of nanocomposite films such as dielectric constant (ϵ), dielectric loss (tanδ) and AC conductivity (
σ
a c
) were also determined as a function of frequency and temperature. Highest ϵ with relatively low tanδ obtained at low frequency for maximum temperature in all polymer nanocomposite films. The
σ
a c
increases from lower (100 Hz) to higher frequency up to 100 kHz and drops to zero for the further increment of frequency. These results suggest that the PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposites are the most promising materials for energy storage applications.

head.ce:title: Synergistic effect of barium titanate nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots on the dielectric properties and conductivity of poly(vinylidenefluoride-co-hexafluoroethylene) films

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs polymer nanocomposite films with good flexibility and high dielectric constant (ϵ) at low frequency were prepared via solution casting technique. Different compositions of BaTiO3 (non-conducting ceramic nanofiller) and Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) (conducting nanofiller) utilized as co-filler were embedded in poly (vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoroethylene) (PVDF-HFP) polymer matrix. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) techniques were employed to characterize the prepared nanocomposite films. The mechanical properties of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films were also investigated. Dielectric properties of nanocomposite films such as dielectric constant (ϵ), dielectric loss (tanδ) and AC conductivity (
σ
a c
) were also determined as a function of frequency and temperature. Highest ϵ with relatively low tanδ obtained at low frequency for maximum temperature in all polymer nanocomposite films. The
σ
a c
increases from lower (100 Hz) to higher frequency up to 100 kHz and drops to zero for the further increment of frequency. These results suggest that the PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposites are the most promising materials for energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:para:
Nowadays, polymers are considering as most frequently used dielectric materials due to their high electric breakdown strength. Polymers are foldable, weightless, low density and can be processed at low temperatures (Mohanapriya et al., 2020). In general, the polymers are incorporated with organic/inorganic fillers for the preparation of polymer nanocomposites to improve their thermal stability, mechanical and dielectric properties (Y. R. Kumar et al., 2021; Josef et al., 2021). The polymer nanocomposites possess excellent dielectric properties such as ϵ, tanδ and
σ
a c
. These advantages of polymer nanocomposites make them promising materials for various potential applications such as in capacitors, batteries, energy storage and smart electronic devices (Tan et al., 2020; Sankaran et al., 2018; Dongre et al., 2019).

Polyvinylidenfluoride (PVDF) has become one of the most significant materials in the field of science and technology due to its excellent properties and applications among polymers (Rani et al., 2021a,b; Rackesh et al., 2018). Recently, its copolymer, so-called PVDF-HFP as emerging material with several advantages compared to PVDF such as low glass transition temperature, high ϵ, small electrical conductivity (σ), high dielectric breakdown strength and porous in nature, better mechanical strength, greater free volume, lower crystallinity, hydrophobic, and high solubility in organic solvents (Ehrhardt et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2019; Sangeetha, 2021; Mohanapriya et al., 2019). These advantages of PVDF-HFP polymer made us to chosen a promising material for potential applications such as self-powering devices, lithium-ion battery and electronic device applications (Rani et al., 2020a,b,c; Chakhchaoui et al., 2021; Gayen et al., 2017).

Recently, the dielectric properties of polymers are enriched by reinforcing the ceramic materials (high relative permittivity) into the polymer matrix using various composite methods (Muzaffar et al., 2021a,b). Among the various ceramic nanoparticles, BaTiO3 has been a widely used ceramic material with the nonlinear ferroelectric response, high dielectric, thermal, and mechanical properties can be achieved by altering the preparation techniques (Kim et al., 2017; Tavangar et al., 2020; Muzaffar et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2016). Further, the BaTiO3 possess several unique properties and due to its several advantages, this ceramic material is essential in multilayer ceramic capacitor, varactor, nonlinear resistor, high-density optical data storage, photocatalytic and transducer applications (Küçük et al., 2018). The BaTiO3 ceramic material shows different crystallographic structures such as tetragonal phases, cubic etc. Generally, the tetragonal crystal structure of BaTiO3 is desired to obtain their ferroelectric and enhanced dielectric properties such as high ϵ and low tanδ (Ahmad et al., 2005). Several research groups reported that BaTiO3 nanoparticles have been used as one of the best nanofiller for the preparation of polymer nanocomposites to enhance their structural, morphological, thermal and dielectric properties (Yang et al., 2013; Bouharras et al., 2020). Further, the polymer nanocomposites incorporated with BaTiO3 nanoparticles have several potential applications in electrostatic capacitors and embedded devices and energy storage (Yu et al., 2013; Reddy et al., 2019). With this inspiration, BaTiO3 nanoparticles are chosen as one of the nanofiller for the preparation of polymer nanocomposites in the present study.

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) is a zero-dimensional conducting nanomaterial that can greatly influence to increase in the ϵ with less quantity. Due to its edge effect and quantum confinement effect, GQDs possess biocompatibility, large surface area, low toxicity and high photoluminescence (Bacon et al., 2014; Thangamani et al., 2021a,b). The GQDs have a capacity of accommodating a large charge-discharge rate, high specific capacitance and high energy storage density. The GQDs have been widely used in energy storage applications because of their attractive excellent thermal and electrochemical properties, as well as good electrical σ and chemical stability. GQDs were used as conducting nanofiller due to their outstanding optical and electrical properties (Kumar et al., 2020; Rani et al., 2020a,b,c; Deshmukh et al., 2020). The electrical properties of polymer composites embedded with nanofillers can be used as various devices such as electrical igniters, heating elements, a crucible for vacuum induction furnace, and electrodes for fuels cells (Theerthagiri et al., 2015a, 2016). The behaviour of the dielectric study as a function of frequency as well as temperature is the most sensitive and suitable approach for studying polymer assembly (Reddy et al., 2020; Remiš et al., 2021; Theerthagiri et al., 2015b, 2021). These interesting properties and advantages of GQDs inspire us to choose one of the nanofiller in our present work for the preparation of polymer nanocomposites.

In the present study, the different contents of conducting (GQDs) and non-conducting (BaTiO3) nanofillers have been utilized as co-fillers and PVDF-HFP as a matrix polymer to prepare flexible PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films. The flexible PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films have been synthesized and explored the Physico-chemical, molecular interactions using various characterization techniques. The mechanical properties of PVDF/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films were also investigated. The temperature and frequency dependence of dielectric properties such as ϵ, tan δ and
σ
a c
were also explored for the prepared polymer nanocomposite films.

PVDF-HFP pellets having an average molecular weight ∼4,00,000 and BaTiO3 nanoparticles (<100 nm) with molecular weight 233.19 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. GQDs with a quantum dots size of nearly 15 nm and thickness between 0.5 and 2 nm was purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories (SRL), India. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) solvent was acquired from SRL, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental procedure for the preparation of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Solution casting is an approachable technique for the preparation of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films. At first, PVDF-HFP was dissolved in DMF (solvent) under stirring at room temperature (RT) for 3 h to become a homogeneous solution. The GQDs solution was directly added to the dispersed PVDF-HFP solution after 3 h and stirred for 1 h to form PVDF-HFP/GQDs colloidal solution or suspension. Simultaneously, BaTiO3 nanoparticles were dispersed in DMF through ultrasound sonication for 30 min. Finally, the dispersed BaTiO3 solution was added with PVDF-HFP/GQDs colloidal solution dropwise. The resultant solution was kept under stirring at 220 rpm for further 3 h to form a homogeneous PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs solution as well as to get rid off the bubbles. Subsequently, the resultant suspension was kept in a hot air oven at 60 °C for 24 h using the Teflon Petri dish. The obtained films were peeled off from the Teflon Petri dish after cooling down to RT. The PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films with different compositions of nanofillers were mentioned in Table 1 . The average thickness of the PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films was ∼70 μm. The schematic illustration of the preparation method of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films is shown in Fig. 1 .

FTIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, IRAffinity-1, Japan) was employed to get FTIR spectra for the pure PVDF-HFP films and PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films embedded with various wt% of nanofillers in the wavenumber range 400–4000 cm−1.

Bruker AXS D8 focus advanced XRD meter (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan) was used to obtain XRD patterns of the prepared samples. The scans were taken from 10° to 90° (2θ range) with Cu–Kα radiation having a wavelength (λ) of 1.54060 Å.
The morphology study of pure PVDF-HFP films and PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films were studied using SEM (JEOL JSM-6480 LV) with an accelerating voltage of 30 kV.

The dielectric studies were performed by using a HIOKI low-frequency LCR meter (IM 3533–01) and a HIOKI High-frequency Impedance analyser (IM 7581) in the frequency range from 100 Hz to 10 MHz. A sample was employed between the electrodes and placed inside the dry temperature calibrator (DPI-1100). Then, the dielectric properties of the samples were characterized by varying ranges of temperatures (30–150 °C) and a broad range of frequency (100 Hz–10 MHz).

Dielectric properties are associated with the storage and dissipation of electrical energy in materials as a function of frequency and temperature. All the nanocomposite films were analysed in the range of 100 Hz to 10 MHz at 30 to 150 °C temperature for every 10 °C of intervals. The ϵ is one of the fundamental electrical properties of solid materials. It is calculated using the following equation:
(1)
ε =
c × d
A ×
ε 0
Where ‘c’ is the capacitance, ‘d’ is the thickness of the polymer nanocomposite films, ‘A’ is the surface area of the films and
ε ο
is the permittivity of air which is represented with the value of 8.854 × 10−12 F/m.

Tanδ is the percentage of an alternating electric field's energy that is converted into heat in the dielectric medium. Generally, the ratio of conduction current and displacement current is called tanδ or conduction loss or damping loss.

The σ of particular material exhibits its capacity to conduct electricity. The alternating
σ
a c
of a sample that is caused by charge carriers moving through a polymer is measured using the following equation.
(2)
σ
a c
= ω ×
ε o
× ε × T a n δ
Where ω is the angular frequency (ω = 2πf),
ε ο
is the dielectric permittivity of free space, and Tanδ is loss tangent (Iqbal and Rafiuddin, 2016).

The FTIR spectra of pure BaTiO3, neat PVDF-HFP polymer, PVDF-HFP/GQDs, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3 and PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films are shown in Fig. 2 . The peak at 1070 cm−1 (non-polar crystalline α phase) corresponds to C–C–C bending and at 1400 cm−1 the CF2 vibration was observed, which are belongs to the PVDF-HFP matrix (Kishor et al., 2020). Also a β phase was shown from the presence of the intense vibrational band at 1230 cm−1. A broad peak at 489 cm−1 was assigned to BaTiO3 which is due to the typical vibrations of Ti–O. Metal-oxygen bonds were observed and are assigned to the band at 406 cm−1. Due to the effect of BaTiO3, the peaks at 400 to 750 cm−1 are suppressed in the polymer nanocomposite (Fig. 2(d-j)), which signifies the BaTiO3 well dispersed in polymer matrix. The Absorption peak at 1433 cm−1 is attributed to stretching of the carbonate ion impurities (Darwish et al., 2010). The stretching vibration of C–H for PVDF-HFP was noticed for the small peaks at 2989 and 3026 cm−1 (Sim et al., 2012). The peak at 871 cm−1 identify the β phase of the PVDF-HFP which indicating the amorphous structure of the polymer and it was shifted to 873 cm−1 in the nanocomposites. Symmetrical stretching mode of CF2 was observed at 1063 cm−1 and for the nanocomposites, it is shifted to 1066 cm−1. The peaks at 1149 and 1173 cm−1 correspond to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibrations of CH2 in the pure PVDF-HFP polymer respectively (Ataollahi et al., 2012). The GQDs incorporated in the PVDF-HFP film was confirmed by the variations that appeared for the peaks in the range from 500 cm−1 to 790 cm−1 in Fig. 2(b) and c. Some of the peaks are disappeared in the polymer nanocomposites which show that the nanofillers and polymer are well mixed (Badatya et al., 2021).

Fig. 3 (a–d) indicates the XRD patterns of the pure BaTiO3, neat PVDF-HFP polymer, PVDF-HFP/GQDs and PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3 nanocomposite films. The XRD pattern of BaTiO3 confirms the cubic phase of BaTiO3 (JCPDS no. #892475). The peaks at 2θ = 22.06°, 32.17°, 38.266°, 44.421°, 49.961°, 55.075°, 64.497°, 69.089°, 73.303° are indexed as (100), (110), (111), (200), (210), (211), (220), (300), (310), diffraction planes of cubic phase respectively (Singh et al., 2017; Gaikwad et al., 2018). Pure PVDF-HFP shows a broad peak at 2θ = 18.35° and 20.17° corresponds to α and β phase, respectively, which indicates the semi-crystalline nature of the polymer (Lu et al., 2019). The inset image shows the zoomed peak of Fig. 3(c) confirms the presence of GQDs at 2θ = 27° corresponding to (002) (Ettefaghi et al., 2017; Tripathi et al., 2019). Fig. 4 (a–f) shows the characteristic peaks of different compositions of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films. There are no large comparative changes in the intensities of each other polymer nanocomposite films except Fig. 4(c), because it may not be proper filler dispersion in the polymer matrix. All the peaks of BaTiO3 nanoparticles were observed to shift slight forward after the composite with fixed PVDF-HFP and different compositions of GQDs.

SEM is one of the powerful techniques which may be useful to get information regarding surface morphology, surface topography and composition of the sample. To investigate the dispersion state of BaTiO3 and GQDs in the PVDF-HFP polymer matrix, SEM studies were carried out. Fig. 5 (a–i) displays SEM images of neat PVDF-HFP film, PVDF-HFP/GQDs, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films loaded with various compositions of GQDs and BaTiO3 nanoparticles respectively, as mentioned in the Table 1. The compressed and locked morphology is observed which indicates the indefinite morphology since the copolymer is used as a matrix for the nanocomposite materials (Oumghar et al., 2020). It is seen that for all the nanocomposite samples, both BaTiO3 nanoparticles and GQDs are uniformly distributed in the PVDF-HFP polymer matrix. However, the aggregation in the fillers has been found in the nanocomposites which indicate the strong interaction between the host polymer matrix and fillers. Also, it is found that there is an increase in surface roughness in the loading content of fillers in the host polymer matrix which could be helpful for the application side.

The mechanical properties of the pristine PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/GQDs, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3 and PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs polymer nanocomposites films were measured by the study of the stress-strain curves as shown in Fig. 6 (a–i). The stress is directly proportional to strain within the range of elastic by the act of force on the material. The values of the mechanical properties such as σ PL , σ y , Young's modulus, σ UTS and (%) elongation at breakpoint are listed in Table 2 . The best results of σ PL , σ y , Youngs modulus and σ UTS were exhibited from the pure PVDF-HFP film than the polymer nanocomposites films. The addition of BaTiO3 and GQDs could be reduced the mechanical properties of PVDF-HFP, even though it retained the flexibility of the film. The pure PVDF-HFP exhibits a maximum σ UTS of 1.13 × 107 MPa and followed by 8.77 × 106 MPa for (PVDF-HFP/GQDs), 7.93 × 106 MPa and 6.78 × 106 MPa for the polymer nanocomposite films, attributed to the low resistance exerted by the fillers. This indicating that the stress and strain performance of the polymer nanocomposite is slightly weaker than that of the pure PVDF-HFP. PVDF-HFP/GQDs polymer nanocomposite which is having a high content of GQDs shows a superior value of % elongation at break when compared to the base polymer matrix. This signifies the GQDs are strong interfacial interactions with PVDF-HFP polymer matrix than BaTiO3 (Thangamani et al., 2021a,b; Zeng et al., 2015).

In the present study, the polymer nanocomposite films are temperature and frequency-dependence of the ϵ analysis were carried out. Fig. 7 shows the ϵ of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films is decreased with a decrease in the BaTiO3 content (wt%) and increase in the GQDs content (wt%) respectively. The highest ϵ was obtained at low frequency (100 Hz) for all the samples at the high temperature of 150 °C (Vaibhav et al., 2018; Muzaffar et al., 2020). With the increase in frequency values, the ϵ of the neat PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/GQDs, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films are decreased due to the interfacial relaxation as well as polarization will not keep with the variation of frequency and gradually vanish one by one (Feng et al., 2015; Deshmukh et al., 2018). The homogenous dispersion of GQDs and BaTiO3 in the PVDF-HFP matrix may be significant enrichment in ϵ at initial frequencies in all polymer nanocomposites (Rani et al., 2021a,b; Reddy et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2021). Further, it is observed that the pure PVDF-HFP nanocomposite shows drastic improvement of ϵ due to more micro capacitors in the format while compared with the remaining samples.

Further, the ϵ studies were carried out for pure PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/GQDs, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films at particular temperatures 40 °C, 100 °C, and 150 °C with different frequencies respectively. At the temperature of 40 °C, PVDF-HFP/GQDs nanocomposite film exhibited the highest ϵ. This is because that ϵ decreases with an increase in frequency and also sudden changes occurred in ϵ at 100 kHz for all the samples at particular temperatures. Table 3 demonstrates that the pure PVDF-HFP film exhibited high ϵ than other nanocomposite films i. e, 16.75, 71.21, 147.07 for the temperatures 40 °C, 100 °C and 150 °C respectively, which attributed charge carriers accumulation between the electrode by the external electrical field. The decrement in ϵ of PVDF-HFP by adding BaTiO3 and GQDs is due to a rapid decrease in interfacial polarization and relaxation of the dipole moment in the polymer chains (Rani et al., 2021a,b). And the ϵ of PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs (90/0/5 wt%) nanocomposite film was considered as second highest in the present work due to the second-highest content of GQDs (5 wt%). It is concluded that conducting GQDs in the polymer nanocomposite films plays a major role to keep up the ϵ high while increasing the content of GQDs.

The tanδ of neat PVDF-HFP, PVDF-HFP/GQDs, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3, PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films decreases with an increase in the frequency at various temperatures as shown in Fig. 8 . It was observed that with an increase in temperature, the tanδ increases for all the samples at low frequencies which may be due to the Maxwell–Wagner effect (Reddy et al., 2019a,b; Rani et al., 2020a,b,c). It is noticeable that tanδ keeps on dropping with an increase in frequency up to 1 MHz for all the samples across the frequency range. Also, an increase in the tanδ further increase in the frequency was observed. The reason behind this is quick destruction decrease due to friction of intermolecular or vibration in the samples. At lower frequencies, the orientation of dipoles during a longer relaxation time contributes to greater tanδ. Because the relaxation time decreases as the frequency rises and then the polarization effect decreases. As a result, intermolecular friction or vibration decreases, resulting in saturated tanδ. On the other hand, tanδ increases beyond 1 MHz for all samples, which might be related to leakage current (Gayen et al., 2017; Thomas et al., 2019). As a result, as shown in Table 3, the entire polymer nanocomposite films exhibit a high ϵ with comparatively low tanδ. These obtained results suggest that the PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films are the most promising materials for energy storage applications.

The plot between conductivities versus frequencies for various compositions of nanofillers in PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films is shown in Fig. 9 . In all the samples the
σ
a c
varies at the lower and higher frequency. The
σ
a c
of the samples increases gradually at low frequencies and hasty increases at higher frequencies. It has a nearly flat portion at lower frequencies and develops to increase in the
σ
a c
values at higher temperatures and frequencies. This is happens because of increment of electronic hopping between localizes states existing inside the nanomaterials. The strong
σ
a c
exhibited for all polymer nanocomposite films up to a 100 kHz and drops down to zero while further increment of frequency, which is attributed to the contribution of ionic conduction grows up to a certain level of frequency (100 kHz). Moreover, the arc of the
σ
a c
spectra are associated with the relaxation of the polymer matrix in the polymer nanocomposite films (Lu et al., 2019). The comparable trend was found in all formats of polymer nanocomposite films except PVDF-HFP/GQDs nanocomposite films because this sample contains high content of conducting GQDs compared to other samples. The
σ
a c
of pure PVDF-HFP is 9.01 × 10−6 Scm−1 and 2.47 × 10−5 Scm−1 at low and high values of frequency respectively for 150 °C temperatures. The influence of GQDs with 5 wt%, the
σ
a c
of PVDF-HFP/GQDs nanocomposites increases to 8.011 × 10−5 Scm−1 and 1.127 × 10−4 Scm−1 at the same frequencies and temperature. The low
σ
a c
exhibit for PVDF-HFP/BaTiO3 with high content of BaTiO3 at low frequency and at high temperature compared to PVDF-HFP/GQDs. The
σ
a c
fluctuation ranges 9.85 × 10−7 – 1.12 × 10−4 S cm−1 were observed in all the films from 50 Hz to 100 kHz as shown in Table 4 . The sudden transformation occurred at frequency level 100 kHz, the
σ
a c
drops from 100 kHz and satisfied. This indicates that the more hops of charge carriers get ineffective in all sample and all functions of temperature which results in dispersive conductivity of polymer nanocomposite (Pasha et al., 2012). The properties of polymer nanocomposite films consist of GQDs can be appropriate for the improvement of various fields of applications and especially in energy storage applications.

PVDF/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films reinforced with various compositions (wt%) of BaTiO3 nanoparticles and GQDs were synthesized using a solution casting approach. All the functional groups present in the polymer nanocomposite films were confirmed with the help of the FTIR spectroscopic technique. XRD patterns revealed the formation of nanocomposite films. The surface morphology of the polymer nanocomposite films was obtained from the SEM analysis and showed that the nanofillers were well dispersed in the polymer matrix. The mechanical properties were also investigated on PVDF/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films, which results in the pure PVDF-HFP has excellent mechanical properties compared to nanocomposite films. Further, the dielectric properties of PVDF/BaTiO3/GQDs polymer nanocomposites showed high ϵ and low tanδ values. Further, the
σ
a c
at low and high frequency at different temperatures showed that at high temperature. From this, it is concluded that the PVDF/BaTiO3/GQDs nanocomposite films embedded with various contents of nanofillers exhibit frequency-dependent electric properties which could be suggested for energy storage applications.
Y. Ravi Kumar -investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation and conceptualization; S. K. Khadeer Pasha -supervision, writing, reviewing, editing, and funding acquisition.
